content:
a single quran habit app is making $80K/month

quranly. unfunded. small uk team.

their pricing: $4.99 to $199.99
my quran app pricing: $0.99/month

i was leaving $96/user/year on the table because i assumed religious users won't pay premium

muslim pro has 62M downloads at $9.99/month with zero marketing spend

stop pricing based on guilt. price based on value delivered.

found 5 zombie AI agents running on my macbook

they were 'killed' in my swarm state file but still had active PIDs burning tokens

33,000 tokens per day. doing nothing.

launchd kept resurrecting them because the plist files were still loaded

lesson: your orchestration layer and your OS process layer are two different things. if you only check one, the other will eat your budget silently.

just fixed a canonical URL mismatch that made an entire website invisible to google

the page existed. it loaded. it had content. google couldn't see it.

the canonical tag pointed to a different URL than the actual page URL

if you have a website: view-source your page. search for rel='canonical'. make sure it matches the URL in your browser.

scraped 146,271 gumroad products

here's where the money actually is (not where you think)

software dominates total revenue at $65.8M. but revenue PER PRODUCT:

writing & publishing: only 226 products. $15,750 revenue per product. highest median sales of any category.

fewer sellers = more money per seller.

yc spring 2026 batch just said it out loud:

'don't sell access to an AI tool for $50/mo. use the AI yourself and sell the finished work for $5,000'

landing page: $500 to client, 2h to build
email sequence: $1,500, 4h
mini-app with onboarding: $3,500, 8h

the client sees agency work. you see a tuesday afternoon.

ran 110 cycles of automated reddit scanning

found 487 niches with zero paid competitors

today's example: personal finance education

r/personalfinance has 23M members. every finance app is a tracker. nobody teaches.

the bluest oceans are hiding inside the most crowded markets. one abstraction layer up.

prompt engineering is dead. the replacement is here.

skill engineering.

MCP ecosystem: 1,000 to 10,000+ servers in 12 months
skills that access live APIs command $100-500/mo per customer
82% of existing MCP servers have security vulnerabilities

a security-audited skill is worth 10x a hacky one.

the next wave of AI money isn't tools. it's skills that run inside tools.

cold email has seasons and nobody talks about this

electrician in ohio. 57-year-old business. godaddy subdomain.

invisible in google during spring renovation season.

'electrician near me' queries spike in march. every year.

'you're invisible during your busiest month' is the entire email.

seasonal timing + specific pain = reply rates above 15%

priced my apps at $0.99/month because i thought 'affordable = more users'

competitor analysis:
- quranly: $4.99-199.99 (making $80K/mo)
- muslim pro: $9.99/mo (62M downloads)
- streaks: $4.99 one-time (top 100)

i was 10x below market. not because my product was worse.

because i priced based on imposter syndrome instead of competitor data.

low price doesn't signal value. it signals 'this probably doesn't work.'

what actually kills most AI automation projects?

- ran out of context window
- OAuth tokens expired silently
- built too much, activated nothing
- nobody checked if it actually worked

(it's the third one. it's always the third one.)

the gap between 'built' and 'making money' is always ________

wrong answers only

confession: i have 524 automation scripts running and $0 in revenue

the scripts work perfectly. the problem is me.

what's the dumbest bottleneck holding YOUR project back?

192,700 leads qualified by automation
17,484 scored as 'hot'
0 contacted

what would you do with a list of 17,000 hot leads and no outreach tool set up?

honest question for anyone building with AI:

are you building a business or building a system that builds a business?

because those are two very different activities and one of them doesn't make money

claude code users: what's your biggest pain point with multi-agent systems?

mine: background agents can't write files (auto-denied permissions)

had to restructure my entire architecture. foreground agents for writes, background for reads.

simple but took me 3 days to figure out.

controversial: religious apps are the most underpriced niche in mobile

quranly: $80K/month, single app, unfunded
muslim pro: 62M downloads, $9.99/month, zero marketing

most devs price religious apps at $0.99 out of weird guilt

underpricing isn't respect. it's leaving value on the table while a competitor serves the same audience at 10x.

82% of MCP servers have security vulnerabilities

same pattern as npm in 2018. move fast, ship insecure, fix later.

except MCP servers handle API keys, customer data, and payment integrations.

easy money: build the same skill everyone else is building, but security-audit it.

security isn't a feature. it's the moat that 82% of competitors just handed you for free.

47 apps live. 22 products built. 524 scripts running. 33 agents on cron. 192,700 leads.

$0 revenue.

the paradox: the more you build, the harder it is to stop building and start selling.

if your system can't receive a dollar from a stranger today, every hour making it 'better' is procrastination disguised as work.

n8n vs zapier vs make -- the answer nobody gives because it kills affiliate commissions:

if you can write python, a $20/month VPS + cron beats all three

i run 524 automation scripts locally. total monthly cost: $0.

zapier would charge approximately $12,000/month for the same volume.

the math on build-in-public nobody does:

tweet about what you built: $0, 2 minutes
facebook ad for same reach: $50-500

the ad disappears when you stop paying
the tweet compounds in search and follower growth forever

build in public isn't a strategy. it's the highest-ROI marketing channel at $0 budget.
